author: @DeeP_BosE
text: the metal shell of a PC cabinet not only houses the mobo  but is also a Faraday Cage. in any other non-metallic material emi filters will be over worked n prone to failure.

replies.author: @HWandW
replies.text: How? I’ve worked on PCs (usually the cheaper ones) that don’t have a metal case. I can understand that a non-metallic case could cause problems from interference, but not component failure. There are “open air” PCs that work just fine. My test rigs are open air. The only time, I would think, a fully enclosed faraday cage would be needed is with an EMF shockwave from a nuclear warhead/ICBM. Maybe it’s a different story for older PCs that were also part of the capacitor plague.

author: @hanslepoeter5167
text:
Nice.

Some comments :

Throwing the harddisk on the table like that is not a good idea. These are sensitive to accelaration and might have errors after such abuse. I once had a customer with a failing harddrive in their server. New system. So he asked me how this was caused. I say mechanical vibrations can cause this. So he guides me into the next room and there's a big hammer drilled hole in the wall drilled when the hd failed.
Placing sensitive electronics on something not conductive will not save you from ESD. The opposite is true. Place them on a conductive surface. Then when you put it down on there touch the conductive surface first with your hand so any potential difference is equalised. Then put the sensitive electronics down on the conductive surface with the other hand. When delivered these mainboards come in a conductive bag. Not in an isolating bag. Putting it on a piece of cardboard is not that bad. cardboard is high impedance but low enough to save you from ESD. Most important is weather conditions for ESD to occur. Dry air is bad. Ever touched another person and had a spark between you and the other ? Took your kids off the trampoline in dry weather conditions ? ESD does only occur in dry weather conditions.

author: @Nuts-Bolts
text:
Looks brilliant! Heath Robinson would be proud of you.
As an aside: Whist one has an old computer open, its a good idea to clean the fluff out of the aluminium heat sinks.  It is not obvious to many, that the reason why their computer one day starts to shut down randomly, is simply because its beginning to overheat due to a build up of fluff. Think of it as preventative maintenance. Compressed air and a soft long hair brush is all one needs. Also, it is possible to look at the CPU temperatures  in BIOS or UEFI to see if they are getting too high (or install an app).  Also, I think a pressure gauge from a welding set or elsewhere would strengthen the steam theme and perhaps a winding handle. Uhm... Suddenly got the itch to explore the boxes in my attic. I may be gone for some time.

author: @wmffgeorge1589
text: My computer is a pavilion quite simular to that one, my one keeps loosing the usb's, and ass they keep freezing whils I'm doing my work such as video editing and designing in cad and such it's getting very enjoying, I'm going to have to sort it out.

replies.author: @Nuts-Bolts
replies.text: Anything involving image processing puts high demand on the CPU etc. Check your temperatures, either in BIOS or   UEFI or better still install an app to monitor all the temps. If the  CPU heat sinks and Graphics cards HS  are full of fluff  clean them out with compressed air and a soft long hair brush.

replies.text:
@Nuts-Bolts yes, well you see it still runs just fine and cary's right on whith whatever it's doing, it's just the usb's that stop working properly, they even stil sense that you've unplugged or plugged something in, just don't transfer any data.
I'm planing on giving it a USB expansion card. Fancks for the help.

author: @hempev
text: You don't use the baking soda "filler" for the superglue?

replies.author: @HWandW
replies.text: The foam board is probably light enough that it shouldn’t matter. But baking soda w/super glue is an awesome idea. I’m not sure the chemical reaction of acrolate & soda; it may get too hot for the foam.

replies.author: @hempev
replies.text: @HWandW My own use of bicarb with CA glue has been hit-and-miss. Needs experimentation!

replies.author: @HWandW
replies.text: @hempev I think it matters which brand of CA. Also sodium bicarbonate can have aluminum in it which may affect the chemical reaction in a good or bad way. I’ve used activated charcoal instead of baking soda.

author: @grahamepigney8565
text:
Wonder whether you can produce a real "steampunk" computer as opposed to a "steampunk" replica computer.

I doubt it, Jules Verne & H.G. Wells had many prescient ideas but until the 1970's computers conformed to the idea that they occupied enormous spaces and required air or water cooling.

Today your phone, perhaps even you watch has far more processing capacity than a 1970s computer let alone one that H. G Wells or Jules Verne predicted.

Back in the '60s it was predicted that the world would only need 7 computers!
